claims.rationale: McKinnon's 2001-2002 intrusion methodology via blank and default passwords across ~97 US military and NASA machines is documented in the UK/US extradition record and his own public interviews.

claims.rationale: McKinnon has repeated this 'non-terrestrial officers' story in interviews since the mid-2000s; the existence of the file itself is attested only by his personal testimony without corroborating documentary evidence.

claims.text: McKinnon interpreted the non-terrestrial officers list as evidence of a secret human-staffed space fleet whose ships did not appear in the US naval registry.

claims.rationale: Conclusion drawn by McKinnon; the video presents it as McKinnon's interpretation of a list, not as independently verified evidence of a space fleet.

claims.rationale: This is Hare's personal whistleblower testimony, widely repeated in UFO circles since the 2001 Disclosure Project but not independently corroborated by NASA records.

claims.text: In the 1960s the US Air Force ran the Manned Orbiting Laboratory (MOL) program in parallel with NASA's Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo programs as a spy station that President Nixon cancelled in 1969.

evidence.quote:

manned orbiting laboratory o

claims.rationale: The MOL program is declassified US Air Force history — it ran 1963-1969, was a crewed reconnaissance station, and was cancelled by the Nixon administration in June 1969. Primary sources include NRO declassified archives.

claims.text: In early 2002 McKinnon was arrested and charged with 97 counts of causing damage to US intelligence assets, accused of $800,000 in damages.

in early 2002 Gary was arrested and

claims.rationale: McKinnon's March 2002 arrest in London and the US indictment on 97 counts with approximately $700k-$800k in claimed damages are part of the public extradition case record (2002-2012).

claims.rationale: Theresa May blocked McKinnon's extradition in October 2012 citing his Asperger's diagnosis and suicide risk — widely reported in UK and US press at the time.

claims.text: The first public reference to a secret US space program named 'Solar Warden' appeared in early 2006 as an anonymous post on the Open Minds forum.

claims.rationale: The 2006 Open Minds forum post is the commonly cited origin of the Solar Warden term in UFO circles; the claim is about authorship and date of a specific post, which can be dated but not externally verified as the absolute first reference.

claims.text: A November 2012 Huffington Post article by reporter Darren Perks was the first major news article about Solar Warden and tied it to Gary McKinnon.

claims.rationale: Perks's November 2012 HuffPost blog entry 'Solar Warden — The Secret Space Program' is verifiable and is the article that introduced the McKinnon-Solar Warden connection to mainstream readers.

claims.rationale: Corey Goode gave sworn deposition testimony in the 2020 Wilcock/Gaia lawsuit that material aspects of his space-program narrative were fabricated; this has been widely reported within the UFO community.

claims.rationale: The narrator is making a source-criticism argument: the Solar Warden tropes match Gerry Anderson's 1970 UFO series closely enough to suggest cultural borrowing, which is plausible but is an interpretive rather than factual claim.
